Hear me out for a second. This new timeout code `deadline - time.time()` means that if the first returned address fails (with a timeout) then it's eaten up all the time and no other addresses are left to try because the timeout has been exhausted. There's no easy fix for this, as a simple backoff algorithm (lets say: dividing the remaining time by `2`) would simply bias the first returned address as having the lions-share of the timeout. I'll leave this as a to-do issue; as, once again, even in this case, it's emulating the existing `[0]` code.
We can use exceptions to signal failure, and need not use return codes for anything.

Ok, after some more reading, I certainly agree with you that `None` should not be returned.

Some sources:
- [MIT 6.0001 Introduction to Computer Science and Programming in Python, Fall 2016 by Dr. Ana Bell](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9H6muyZjms0&t=1678). On handling exceptions by substituting with a default value: "[T]he user thinks that they entered something, and they think everything's great, your program accepts it, but then they get some weird value as an output, which is far from what they expected. So it's not really a good idea to just replace users' values with anything."
- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/1630706/best-practice-in-python-for-return-value-on-error-vs-success
- https://github.com/williballenthin/python-registry/issues/76
- https://wiki.python.org/moin/HandlingExceptions

In our discussion in issue [#171](https://github.com/pydot/pydot/issues/171#issuecomment-612582791), I worried that some users may depend on a possible return value `None`. In other words, removing that may be a breaking change. Some remarks about that:
- I checked the current pydot 1.4.1 code and did not see anything directly or indirectly depending on a return value of `None` from this function. I also checked [NetworkX 2.4](https://github.com/networkx/networkx/tree/networkx-2.4) code as an example of a library that depends on pydot and also did not find any there. (There is of course a multitude of dependent code out there that I did not check.)
- The current docstring of the function states that it returns Graphs and does not mention possibly returning `None`.
- Still, I think changes in return values (and possible exceptions) are too far-reaching for a mere point release (e.g. 1.4.2). So, I will split off the bug fix for the `TypeError` to a separate PR (as soon as we agree on the solution for that). This PR (219) will keep the more substantial changes, including the removal of `return None`, for the next major or minor release (e.g. 1.5.x). I will add a Changelog-entry and any necessary function docstring changes to this PR in an effort to make users aware of the change.

> Wouldn't be better to add a new package-level config-option (e.g. `pydot.raise_errors`), not to ever return `None`, but always let exceptions bubble-up?

If we agree on removing `return None`, then that config-option is not necessary anymore, right? I mean, if the function does not have a Graph object to return (substitution by an empty Graph is not good either) and it should not return `None`, then what else can we do in the end except raise an exception (whether it be our own or bubbling up the original), right?

Hope this gets some things out of the way already. I will still get back on several other issues we were discussing (error handling, logging, printing, flags, etc.), either here or in our parallel discussion in issue #171.

It's `[a, b, c, d, e, f, g]` -- row 0 of the fork table.  This is because the parent of `k` is `b`.  `[a, b, k, l, m]` is not a fork row, because it is not an entry in the fork table.

The fork table is constructed by appending blocks to the end of an existing fork row if possible, and only creating a new fork row if this is impossible.  Supposing that the blocks were mined in order `a,b,c,d,e,f,g,h,i,j,k,l,m`, then the fork table would contain 1 row for `a,b,c,d,e,f,g` because each subsequent block up until `g` was able to be appended to an existing fork row.  When block `h` arrives, it would be put into its own fork row, because it is not a child of `g`.  Blocks `i` and `j` would go into `h`'s fork row, because `i` is the child of `h` and `j` is the child of `i`.  When block `k` arrives, it would be added to its own fork row because it cannot be appended to the fork row containing its parent, `b`.

@kantai I agree with @zone117x on this one -- there are a plethora of on-chain tokens that will have a total supply, and making it hard for them to screw up enforcing a total supply would be beneficial to everyone.

But regardless of how a total supply is enforced, this discussion nevertheless raises the related question of how we intend to support tokens that do _not_ have a total supply, if at all.  We currently don't support the notion of "infinite" tokens.  I personally don't think we should try to do so, since trying to do so introduces the problem of keeping the number of bytes required to encode absurdly large numbers under control.  But if we don't support "infinite" tokens, then every token already implicitly has a "total_supply" of 2**127 - 1.  Given this, I think we're already forcing a choice about token supplies work -- it's just that we're doing so implicitly if we don't offer a `total_supply` field.

Thanks for the feedback.  Yeah, that makes sense.  I've gone back and forth on this.  It's about who has the burden of responsibility to make sure wheels pass.  If they fail, I think it's more likely that something changed with cibuildwheel or GA than being caused by the PR.  In this case, I don't want to frustrate contributors via long CI cycles _and_ having them worry about wheels.

The current strategy in this PR is:
- Build wheels for all commits to main branch and for release tags, but not for PRs
  - Hence, we'll know if a PR breaks wheels after it's merged; I'll investigate and ping the author if appropriate
- Upload all wheel and sdist artifacts to github as part of GA and retain for 7 days
- Upload wheel and sdist to PyPI for tags only

I'd like to try this for now, and I'm open to changing strategies if we find that PRs sometimes cause wheels to fail.

A few responses:
* boolean params with sceptre are tricky. Because of the way we parse our vars, if you passed `--var exclude=false`, that would actually resolve to `"false"` which is actually truthy.
* `!stack_attr` doesn't have access to `var`, since `var` isn't a stack attribute; it's available for jinja rendering, so you'd have to do `{{ var.use_test_resources }}`
* Technically, it wouldn't be a global exclude. `create`, `update`, and `delete` will still act upon the stack just as normally. This is only an instruction for the broader, "smarter" `launch` command. Calling it "exclude" I think would less clear about what it actually does. I want the user to be able to explicitly act upon stacks as desired without really breaking the way those commands work. `launch`, however, is intended as a "apply my current configuration" command that already will perform creates, updates, and deletes based upon the current configuration and the deployed configuration.

The default launch_action, as I've configured it in this PR is `include`, so unless someone overrides that, it'll work the same as usual. So then the difference is between `launch_action: "exclude" # or maybe "skip"` and `exclude: true`.

Yes, lets just target the currently supported contexts. We can swing back around later when we add support dynamic loading of contexts. We'll make sure to implement something generic in the PR (ie. setting context with string rather than enum) so it will just work. 

> Currently the dispatch logic is inside FCompute, which is a bit different from existing mxnet users' experience. Usually the FCompute only declares the computation, and leave the dispatch logic to MXNet executor. And it's unclear how it supports the case where the same op is extended by a library for Intel CPUs and NVIDIA GPUs - they may hard-code the dispatch logic to only care about their own hardware. How do we handle such conflicts?
 
Good catch. We'll change it so that users can specify context and Forward/Backward function in the registration. But, for custom operators we can only support what the current implementation in MXNet allows. Which is that the top level scope is an operator, and it has implementations for different contexts.

What you're describing is top level being context and inside of that having a distinct operator registration. Like you describe next, this organization is not supported in MXNet. We should discuss this as part of a separate feature enhancement than this PR (the scope of this PR is to add GPU support to custom operators -- only). 

> Furthermore, currently the infer_shape/infer_dtype is not context-aware, i.e. CPU and GPU infers the same dtype. However, it may not be true (e.g. cpu supports fp32 and bfloat16, and gpu supports fp32 and fp16). How do we handle these attribute conflict?
> 
> I had a short discussion with @yzhliu and we saw two potential fixes:
> 
> 1. make infer_shape/infer_dtype context aware. This way we can have different infer_dtype function for cpu & gpu. MXNet needs to dispatch to the function based on the current context. For example, `op.set_attr<FInferType>("FInferType<cpu>", my_infer_type_function)` for cpu specific type inference, and `op.set_attr<FInferType>("FInferType<gpu>", my_infer_type_function_gpu)`for gpu.
> 2. Another way is to register ops with different names (e.g. 'cpu_gemm' and 'gpu_gemm'). This way they can have different infer_attr functions. But we don't want users to modify their model definition in the training script to these names. To mitigate that we can have an API to allow user to provide a mapping (e.g. {'gemm' -> 'cpu_gemm'}) for mxnet to map an op to another op registered in the backend.

This is a good idea, we should have a separate PR to add this new feature to MXNet backend, and then extend it out to custom operators. But this is out of scope for this PR. 

Another point is that current custom operator support does not allow for registering CPU implementations in one library, and GPU implementations for the same operator in another. This merging of functionality from different libraries is a good idea for a future feature.

> Finally, is there a plan to support dynamic custom context? :P @samskalicky

> i presume this means that resourceStates is mutable and changes over time. Is htat ok? are there any concerns about the semantics of that as we're running the user program? i'm just always nervous about accessing shared mutable state

Yes - it is mutable (and fundamentally needs to be mutable as we want to look up the current registration of a URN at a point in time).  There is certainly a contract here that our attempt to look it up happens "after" the resource is registered, and we are relying on the idea that awaiting all the channel/rpc/promise steps on the registration will ensure that the place we do the lookup is indeed "after".  It is certainly possible that there is some potential issue here - but I don't know exactly what it would be. Can we consider using `enum : uint8_t` for these constants instead of `static constexpr`?

The advantage of `enum` is that we know for sure that it would not be placed in flash/ROM by any tool-chain  (should be just treated a named constant value during compile time).

With a `static constexpr` variable, some tool-chains may choose to allocate a memory location (in flash/ROM) to store the value (which will unnecessarily impact/increase code-size). Even though I expect most tool-chain not to do this, I don't think we can be sure.

I know we did similar logic in original implementation (without considering the priority). But thinking more about this, I think it may be better if we first try to remove a message at same priority level as `aPriority`.

How about if we start the search from `GetHeadForPriority(aPriority)` (to start from message with same priority as `aPriority`). ```py
```py
>>> import re
>>> match = "'font' is not set."
>>> re.escape(match)
"'font'\\ is\\ not\\ set\\."
```

Yes -- I think that this is not so much about whether or not "accidental trait implementation" is absolutely required in all cases (it's not), but more about enabling a common case of implementation --- basically one smart contract defines a interface without knowing that it may be generally useful for other contracts, and then other contracts come along and implement it as a trait. If we forced explicit trait implementation, for the first contract to be used as a trait, it would need to relaunch, which isn't ideal.

It's true that implementing traits like this requires a dynamic check. However, it doesn't actually require a check at the site of the `contract-call?`, instead, it just need to be performed on `ClarityBlockConnection::run_contract_call`, since these concretizations can only be passed in as transaction arguments.

```
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import numpy as np
from numpy.testing import assert_allclose

def mean_squared_distance(input, target, reduction='mean', w=None):
    out = np.square(input - target)
    if w is not None:
        out = np.multiply(out, w)
    if reduction == 'mean':
        out = np.mean(out)
    elif reduction == 'sum':
        out = np.sum(out)
    return out

loss = nn.MSELoss()
input = torch.randn(3, 5)
target = torch.randn(3, 5)
output = loss(input, target)

my_loss = mean_squared_distance(input.numpy(), target.numpy())
assert_allclose(my_loss, output.numpy(), rtol=1e-05)
```

SDB file has meta info in it:

```
self.meta = {
  'schema': [
    {'content': 'speech', 'mime-type': 'audio/opus'},
    {'content': 'transcript', 'mime-type': 'text/plain'},
  ],
}
```

Then it's read like this:

```python
self.schema = self.meta['schema']
self.speech_index = self.find_column(content='speech')
self.audio_type = self.schema[self.speech_index]['mime-type']
self.transcript_index = self.find_column(content='transcript')
self.text_type = self.schema[self.transcript_index]['mime-type']
```

And `find_column` is:

```python
def find_column(self, content):
  for index, column in enumerate(self.schema)
      if column['content'] == content:
        return index
  return -1
```

This means only the first entry for a given `'content'` will ever be read. So we're effectively reading this as a dictionary where a key can only have one value, but it's represented as a list of tuples. Maybe add a warning or error message if more than one entry is found for a given key? That would at least avoid people thinking we support more than we do.

Good question. There are several ways a `country_hint` can be set for your batch, in order of what takes precedence:

1) Individually add a `country_hint` to each item in the batch by passing input as a list of dict, e.g. `[{"id": "1", "country_hint": "US", "text": "this is written in english"}, ...]`
2) Pass `country_hint` at the operation-level (each item will use this hint)
3) Set the `default_country_hint` at the client level (If user doesn't pass (1) or (2), then each item uses this hint)

The `language` concept for the other methods works the same way. The inputs do not need to be in the same language, but if a user does not specify a `country_hint` the service will use "US" as the default (most of their customer base). We recently added the option of passing a `country_hint="none"` if you don't want the service to use that default.

Technically, `has_option` is doing more work than necessary by relying on the `get_value()` implementation, e.g. it will interpolate values and `_stringify` them. But, I think that's fine to make this method much simpler. Otherwise, it has extremely high duplication of `get_value()`.

description: Ability to spike an Event

label: Spike
description: User can spike items

label: Spike - View
description: Use can see spiked items

Later on when refactoring we can add the Superdesk module or group by any other means. But simply adding in a column in the UI to show the description should be enough for now.

I still prefer to use float64 as the default type in the function argument instead of having a checker at the beginning of the function. The reason is that the type of `dtype` would be `Union[None, str, numpy.dtype]` in the current implementation, which is inconsistent with the docstring saying that  `dtype : str or numpy.dtype`. Also, it makes the semantic ambiguous and should be avoided especially in Python 3.7. If the default value of `dtype` in the argument is `np.float64` or 'float64' then we can enforce its type to be `Union[str, numpy.dtype]` as documented.

I can change the other to raising an exception if wanted. I would like to make a single point before I do, you may change your mind on it. Exception catching is expensive compared to boolean evaluation.

I used this script
```python
import timeit

code1 = """\
test = True

def send():
    return test

while not send():
    pass

while not send():
    pass

"""

code2 = """\
test = True

def send():
    if not test:
        raise RuntimeError()

try:
    send()
except RuntimeError:
    pass

"""

print(timeit.timeit(code1, number=1000000))
print(timeit.timeit(code2, number=1000000))
```

and these are the results for running each one 1000000 times
boolean = 0.337939405
exception = 0.42874530899999996

running this test 1000000 times may seem excessive, it's not. take a vehicle for instance, say there are 20 OBDII PIDS that I want to collect data from every 5 milliseconds. that's 4000 messages a second, 240000 messages a minute and after only 5 minutes of the application running we are at 1200000. with a time difference of 90 milliseconds that ends up costing 1.296 seconds every hour the application runs. after 24 hours it's at 31 seconds.

using the exception catching way you end up with a double whammy because you need to evaluate the returned value in order to decide whether or not you want to raise the exception nd in the user code exception catching needs to be put into place, where as we are not evaluating anything in the send method.

I know it seems trivial but it really adds up over time

 - Autosaving anything not-yet-written-to-disk on `find_dir` as it also looks for new `Include` and `IncludeOptional` directives - causing a reload of Augeas parsing tree, and erasing all unsaved changes.
 - "Manually" added parse paths to include `VirtualHost` files from arbitrary location using `--apache-vhost-path`

We don't have this check in most places. It is necessary only if we pass an argument that results in some computation. For example, for
```python
_logger.debug("xyz %s", costly_call())
```
the check makes sense. But for
```python
_logger.debug("xyz %s", some_reference)
```
it is not necessary because the logging package is smart enough to do string interpolation lazily only if the logger is enabled for that log level.

But thanks to your comment, I saw that we were using lazy interpolation only on some places, there were lots of non-lazy interpolations of the form
```python
_logger.debug("xyz %s" % some_reference)
```

Investigating further: what formerly was modeled as an `ExtSlice` is now modeled as a `Tuple` containing (one or more) `Slice` items. This implies that `Tuple` should get the treatment of `ExtSlice`.

Python 3.6:
```
>>> from ast import parse, dump
>>> print (dump(parse("l[1, 2:3]", mode="eval")))
Expression(body=Subscript(value=Name(id='l', ctx=Load()), slice=ExtSlice(dims=[Index(value=Num(n=1)), Slice(lower=Num(n=2), upper=Num(n=3), step=None)]), ctx=Load()))
```

Python 3.9:
```
>>> from ast import parse, dump
>>> print (dump(parse("l[1, 2:3]", mode="eval"), indent=4))
Expression(
    body=Subscript(
        value=Name(id='l', ctx=Load()),
        slice=Tuple(
            elts=[
                Constant(value=1),
                Slice(
                    lower=Constant(value=2),
                    upper=Constant(value=3))],
            ctx=Load()),
        ctx=Load()))
```
However, almost surely, the method must change significantly (if it is still necessary at all): beside `Name`, `Constant` and `Tuple` almost any expression type can arrive as the following (3.9) example demonstrates:
```
>>> print (dump(parse("l[a+b, 2:3]", mode="eval"), indent=4))
Expression(
    body=Subscript(
        value=Name(id='l', ctx=Load()),
        slice=Tuple(
            elts=[
                BinOp(
                    left=Name(id='a', ctx=Load()),
                    op=Add(),
                    right=Name(id='b', ctx=Load())),
                Slice(
                    lower=Constant(value=2),
                    upper=Constant(value=3))],
            ctx=Load()),
        ctx=Load()))
```
This implies that it must handle all those types.

Do we still know why this transform was introduced? I have the hope that it may no longer be necessary.

If it is still necessary, we likely should change the logic: handle known cases which require actions (`Tuple`, `Slice`, `ExtSlice`, `Index`) and return everything else unchanged.

At this point in the code, `position_filename_` is something other than `""`. That means users included it in `params`.

It's pretty clear in that case that the user's intent was "load positions from a file".

In my opinion, failing with a big loud error here is preferable to a warning and just continuing training without the positions data. I know I personally would be annoyed if I launched a training job that took a few hours to run, and only at the end realized that it didn't use position bias adjustments because I provided a path to a file that didn't exist.

I'm also recommending quoting the filename with single quotes. That way, if the user happened to provide a filename that also looks like a plausible word in this log message, it'll be clearer that the log message is refering to user-provided configuration. Consider the following.

```text
# no quotes
[INFO] Position file size is not found.

# quotes
[INFO] Position file 'size' is not found.
```

It's a minor thing, but I think it's helpful.

Structurally this is sound, however, I'd like to request a refactor to the ordering of things.  Rather than doing all the setup and then checking whether all of the values are present, I'd prefer we invert this and do the checks first, trigger an early exit if there is a mis-configuration, and only set the values if everything is configured correctly.

For example, I believe the following are the error conditions:

- no `server_url` provided and `network_id` is not in known list.
- `--ethstats` was enabled but not `server_secret` was provided.

The modified function would look something like:

```python
if not args.ethstats:
    return

if not args.ethstats_server_url and self.context.trinity_config.network_id not in DEFAULT_SERVER_URLS:
    self.logger.error("You must provide the ethstats server url using the `--ethstats-server-url` option")
    self.context.shutdown_host()
    return
elif not args.ethstats_server_secret
    self.logger.error("You must provide secret for ethstats using `--ethstats-server-secret`")
    self.context.shutdown_host()
    return

# now we know we have everything we need and can set the various values on `self`
````

By pre-validating we avoid a certain family of errors which can easily show up in subsequent refactors.

Bad news incoming:

It looks like `connect-azuretables` doesn't seem to support SAS signatures, which is what we use for controlling access to azure resources.  Providing a storage account and access key means that the application can access any table in that account -- including the secrets service's table, for example.  No bueno..

I also suspect that we will be wanting to add API methods to manage sessions soon (sec folks will want a way to revoke all sessions for a user), and this library's use of a private table wouldn't permit that.

I also suspect that we'll be adding additional Azure tables in web-server, to support the third-party logins process (tables of access_tokens, for example).  So using the existing TC azure infrastructure will make sense.

One option here would be to modify the `connect-azuretables` library to accept a function that returns a SAS signature, which is conveniently what [tc-lib-azure](https://github.com/taskcluster/taskcluster/tree/master/libraries/azure#readme) produces and what [azure-entities expects](https://github.com/taskcluster/azure-entities#sas-function).

I looked through the code of `_can_read` and `_can_write` and I would like to optimize/change it in a way that it no longer relies on hardcoded formats to choose how to initialize

https://github.com/imageio/imageio/blob/6fbab81e7598af847c840c93c9ebec9a94d1f242/imageio/plugins/pillow.py#L97

Instead, I want it to run `Image.preinit()`, check if the format can be supported, and if not run `Image.init()` and try again. This way, we automatically match pillow's behavior if they change the formats included in `preinit()`. (For example, `.tiff` used to be pre-initialized, but that is no longer the case). This is also what `Image.open` does under the hood.

The second check we do in _can_read/_can_write is to check if our plugin has a matching, loaded plugin inside pillow 

https://github.com/imageio/imageio/blob/6fbab81e7598af847c840c93c9ebec9a94d1f242/imageio/plugins/pillow.py#L106

https://github.com/imageio/imageio/blob/6fbab81e7598af847c840c93c9ebec9a94d1f242/imageio/plugins/pillow.py#L123

In the current implementation, this is the only place where we need knowledge of available pillow plugins; which would be a priori knowledge, since we need to create the correct id string. It will be problematic if a user is using custom pillow plugins, since we wouldn't be able to detect them. A better way would be to iterate over all currently loaded formats and see if they can support our file/uri. This (+ some extra legwork) is again what `Image.open(...)` does under the hood.

Another problem here is that our current _can_read/_can_write do not respect pillow's `formats=...` option. The option essentially allows excluding some pillow plugins, and if a user passes `imopen("some.png", plugin="pillow", formats=["PNG", "JPEG"]` I would like to do the expected thing and honor that if possible. I am happy to update the current implementation, but this is again a dublication of pillow's `Image.open`.

This brings me to the crux of the problem, which is our desire to read `getfirstbytes` once and reuse it for each of pillow's plugins. Internally, `Image.open` does this already; it reads the beginning of the file, stores it, and then passes that to each of it's plugins. The only gain I can see from using `getfirstbytes` instead is that we don't have to read the first 16 bytes if pillow wasn't the first backend to be tested. Somehow I doubt this will be a performance bottleneck.

The other topic is non-seekable files, because pillow expects a `seek`able object. For this, our Request object wraps any non-seekable file into a `Request.SeekableFileObject`, which essentially adds an in-memory cache to make the object seekable and available to other backends later on.

So the bottom line, regarding can_read, is that - from my current perspective - it duplicates most of the current Image.open. That said, it could be that I am missing something here.

Regarding _can_write, I agree that it can be useful to check the supported formats upon initialization and fail if there is no compatible one. I wonder if pillow can already do this for us. I will investigate more.

Actully thinking about this, we should never get to situation to re-use the same `mFinalizeMessage` instance.

If `SendMessage()` is successful, we immediately set the `mFinalizeMessage` to `NULL`.

The case to cover is when `SendMessage()` returns an error. When we get to this situation and call `SendJoinerFinalize()` we are already at `OT_JOINER_STATE_CONNECTED` state and we have a timeout timer running. The only way forward is either being `Stop()`ed (which would clear/free the message) or timer timing out (which again free the message from `Finish()`).

So a `mFinalizeMessage` instance will only be used once.

OK. I tried very hard, and finally found an insane corner case. I am not sure that it worth to study it, but let's expose it.

Let's consider that the real path of certbot-auto is `/root/certbot/certbot-auto`. Let's consider also that we have a symlink `/tmp/certbot` pointing to `/root/certbot` AND some insane admin sys executed the command `chown -h myuser:myuser /tmp/certbot` (or he created the symlink as a normal user, not using sudo ...).

Let's consider now:

1) That certbot-auto is called through `/tmp/certbot/certbot-auto`.
In this case, the input from `"$0"` is `/tmp/certbot/certbot-auto`, and path resolution here will be `/tmp/certbot/certbot-auto`. When checking `/tmp/certbot`, so on the symlink owned by `myuser` pointing to `/root/certbot` owned by `root`, will raise a `PermissionDenied` (even running as root, as the permissions between the symlink and its target are not corresponding.

2) That certbot-auto is called from `/tmp/certbot` using `./certbot-auto`
In this case, the input from `"$0"` is `./certbot-auto` and path resolution is `/root/certbot/certbot-auto` because `abspath` will resolve the symlinks. It means that the permissions will be seen as safe. But `/tmp/certbot` is owned by `myuser`, that can delete the symlink and point it to somewhere else.

In the first situation, I think that we should check if the current path is in fact a symlink, and check its ownership. In the second situation, I do not know how to get the path including symlinks from bash `"$0"` variable.

> I'm not sure about offsets purpose.

Save work. Some of the windows in the `windows` array are not valid. 

For example, if you have a `img.shape=(8,8)` image and a `kernel.shape=(1,3)` kernel, then you pad the image `img_padded.shape=(10,8)` and attach a window in the top-left corner instead of the center. However for `padded_img[0,7]` there is no window because the kernel doesn't fit. However, looking at the image in memory it is still possible to extract `window = padded_img.ravel[7:7+kernel.size]` and apply a kernel.  The window will simply "wrap" around the array. The result of the computation is quite meaningless though, and we would discard/ignore it later.

Instead of doing the above, we can skip those "invalid" windows. Hence the introduction of `offsets` which skips those computations.

The interpreter interface for `read_analog_f64` will need to take `read_array` by reference, so that clients can pass in a preallocated numpy array. However, the gRPC message `ReadAnalogF64Request` does not have a `read_array` field; that is only on the `ReadAnalogF64Response`. The gRPC interpreter implementation of `read_analog_f64` will have to assign `read_array` to have the value of `response.read_array`, while being careful to modify the passed-in ndarray rather than rebind it to point to the `response.read_array` field. I think you might want something like `read_array[:] = response.read_array`, *not* `read_array = response.read_array`.

I think the SRP server should bind to Thread interface.

It doesn't make much sense for the SRP server to accept registrations
from both Thread network and Wi-Fi/Ethernet networks. At least, there
is currently no such use case I think.

Besides, I think the SRP server (and the DNSSD server) should not bind a
platform UDP socket at all because they don't work on both networks. The
only case that I see platform UDP is explicitly required is the Border Agent
that connects external Commissioner and the Leader (and Joiner). For other
general UDP services, I think the expected default behavior is that they should
only work in Thread network unless explicitly required (we may have a flag in
`Udp::Socket` that indicates whether we want to bind to a platform UDP). I think
it is safer and simpler in this way (e.g. we don't need to explicitly filter out TMF
and MLE ports) ,  Thoughts?

When the OIDC issuer URL is not specified in settings, it is generated on the fly.  It is generated by OAuth2Validator.get_issuer_url(), where we don't have a django request, only an oauthlib request, and the oauthlib request lacks the context of whether the original django request was made over SSL or not.

This leads to it generating an incorrect URL for SSL sites (something like `http://mysite.com:443/o/`). To combat this, we set an additional header in to note whether django considers the request is being made over HTTPS or not, and pull this out when constructing the new URL.

My understanding is that when using placement groups with Tune, the first bundle is for the Trainable and any subsequent bundles will be used for additional actors that are launched by the trainable. When using RaySGD with Tune, the driver creates the Tune Trainables, and each Tune Trainable instantiates a `TorchTrainer` which creates `num_workers` actors. In the case where `use_local` is True, the first training worker will be on the same process as the Trainable, so the Trainable needs enough resources to launch a worker and the current code works for that case. However when `use_local` is False, all of the workers are remote actors, so the Trainable itself should only request 1 CPU any not any GPUs. So I believe the bundles for the `use_local` False case should be `[{"CPU": 1}] + [{"CPU": cpus_per_worker, "GPU": int(use_gpu)}] * num_workers`

We should keep requests separate from allocated resources. 

To think of it in pseudo C terms:

```
// Resource requests
int rq1 = 5
int rq2 = 5

assert(rq1 == rq2)

// Allocated memory
ar = malloc(rq1)

// ... use memory allocated in ar somewhere

free(ar)
```

For a more high-level explanation:

If resource state is tied to the request (e.g. via a property), we lose the equality of two different resource objects with the same specs. This in turn would mean that we lose the flexible association of resources to actors. And this in turn would mean that Tune can't control which trials it wants to run first.

Another reason is that it would change the ownership contract. A ResourceRequest is a declarative statement. It is associated to trials (as a PGF), it can be shipped around the cluster, copied, is part of several APIs. The allocated resource however is a singleton. Using a resource manager at all means.

I'll expand the resource manager docstring a bit

In general `Hash32(block_slot_or_hash)` is to be preferred as this checks the type and e.g. `Hash32("foo")` will be rejected by mypy. In contrast nonsense such as `cast(Hash32, "foo")` is allowed as a cast will accept anything you feed it.

However, in this specific case, unfortunately the type hints don't have any affect at all. That is because `functool.partial` erases any type hints, everything becomes `Any` and we are left without any type safety. E.g. you can try replacing `cast(Hash32, block_slot_or_hash)` with `"foo"` and it will go through even though `get_block_by_hash` should not accept a string.

My preference would be to rewrite this part in a less dynamic way (reject wrong types, then execute one or the other)

I think that's the simplest solution: the exporter class itself doesn't own the queue, and may even be stateless if you don't need e.g. to persist spans to disk. Each exporter comes with a factory for initializing the queue and worker.

In this case the tracer would send spans to multiple queues, one for each exporter. E.g. as `SpanQueueRegistry` here:

```
exporter package:
   # create the queue, worker, and exporter 
  (Exporter, SpanQueue) get_exporter(config) 
  
  class Exporter
    # called by the worker thread
    export(list<Span>)

class SpanQueueRegistry
  # add spans to all queues to be exported
  void add_spans(list<Span>) 

# tracer adds spans to queue instead of calling export directly
Tracer(SpanQueueRegistry)
```

``refdata`` was a term we used early on which stands for "Reference Data".  This was intended to be things related to jobs, like "machine" or "option collection" or "Job Type", etc.  

We used to use the [``ReferenceDataSignatures``](https://github.com/mozilla/treeherder/blob/7fd8bd2e00c9b0519b9a342dfceb330eeea76f9b/treeherder/model/models.py#L376) to allow sheriffs to hide certain jobs they wanted to ignore.  We don't do that anymore, however.  Now we just assign them to Tier-3, so they're hidden by default.  

We DO still create that signature to help users filter the list of jobs more specifically than by just the text strings (see the ``Job`` field in the left side of the details panel for a ``sig`` link.

There's lots more history I could give you, but, it's really moot at this point.  :)  The "Reference Data" term could be removed if it was cumbersome in any way.  It's just a general naming convention.

@hoytak , @Jarvi-Izana , and I discussed this at length and here are our thoughts:

1. For `output_type="probability"` in a binary classifier setting, all our supervised learning toolkits currently follow the approach of sorting labels lexicographically, and returning the probability of the last label.
2. This approach is kind of arbitrary, since the labels could be "cat" and "not cat", in which case the user will end up with probabilities for the label, "not cat".
3. After some discussion, we came to the conclusion that for TC 6.0, we should modify this behavior across Drawing Classifier, Logistic Classifier, SVMs, and other supervised learning toolkits to the following: `output_type="probability"` should only apply if the target values in the data are `0` and `1`. This issue is tracked in #2667 .

Here is what I suggest w.r.t this PR:

1. Let's merge this PR in its current state where it matches what we currently have for all supervised learning toolkits.
2. Let's address #2667 before TC 6.0 and make a code change in this logic (and elsewhere) then.

@nickjong @srikris @Jarvi-Izana @hoytak Thoughts?

1. 

```python
Tuple[int, int, int]
```

2.

```python
Tuple[int, ...]
```

To summarize here, we have observed the need for `msvcrt` locking because we have seen Python allow us to open the same file in multiple processes on Windows.

For deleting a file opened by another process, I'd love to see a simple statement in Microsoft documentation like:

> It is impossible to delete a file opened by another process across all versions of Windows unless X, Y, or Z.

I don't think such a statement exists, however, we have observed that Python does not allow us to do that with our current code and dependencies and we've seen Python developers talking about how:

> On Windows this is not possible for files opened using io.open() or os.open().

Given that, I think this code is correct with the current Python and Windows APIs.

On a side note, I noticed that the API design is a little flawed (every design is somehow) in that it will inevitably open the file during plugin construction to identify the plugin that needs to be loaded. This is mildly annoying, because it happens outside the context manager, which could leak resources if the program crashes between instantiation of the plugin and creation of the context.

I think for now this is something we can live with (it was like this before), but in the long run, I don't know what the best way to solve it is. We could return a `LazyPlugin`, where common initialization is done, and then - on the first method call, which should happen inside a context manager - it evolves into a real plugin or raises an exception.

An alternative (may be easier) is to simply closes the file when we are done initializing and re-open it inside the context manager. This is pretty simple, but opens the file twice, which isn't ideal.

In either case, I think this is something for a new PR to fix

Have you had a chance to think much about how the serialization/deserialization is going to work?

We likely need to add a marker attribute [in the SDK](https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/master/sdk/dotnet/Pulumi/Serialization/Attributes.cs), something like:

```c#
namespace Pulumi
{
    [AttributeUsage(AttributeTargets.Class)]
    public sealed class EnumTypeAttribute : Attribute
    {
    }
}
```

That we can then annotate on these like:

```c#
[EnumType]
public readonly struct ContainerColor : IEquatable<ContainerColor>
{
    ...
}
```

So we can detect these during serialization/deserialization and do the proper thing to convert to/from the underlying string.

We'd only have to annotate these structs... the ones that are actual enums (e.g. `public enum ContainerSize`), we can already detect via [`Type.IsEnum`](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.type.isenum?view=netcore-3.1).

Here are the requirements for a struct-based enum to be able to be serialized/deserialized. It must:

 - Be a value type (`struct`) decorated with `EnumTypeAttribute`
 - Have a constructor that takes a single parameter of the underlying type (the constructor can be `private`)
 - Have an explicit conversion operator that converts the enum type to the underlying type
 - Have an underlying type of `String` or `Double`

Implementing `IEquatable`, adding `==`/`!=` operators, and overriding `ToString` isn't required, but is recommended and is what our codegen will do.

We could definitely use `std::function` --- that would be just fine.  I avoided it in part because pybind11 currently doesn't use `std::function` except in the separate, not default-included header, `pybind11/functional.h`.

I don't think there is really any situation in which `std::function` is more efficient or faster to compile or produces smaller generated code than `function_view`:

https://godbolt.org/z/57rMnnjTT

But in any case, I am happy to change to `std::function` if that is preferred.  Certainly if we don't anticipate any further uses of `function_view` (basically equivalent to `absl::FunctionRef` by the way) within pybind11 then it is probably not worth adding it for this purpose.

Due to Helm convention of producing chart packages that follow the pattern `[chart_name]-[chart_version].tgz`, the `output_path` used here is not interpreted as a file name (as other `package` goal implementations do), but rather as a directory name.

This can be confusing to users as it is a _special case_ for the `output_path` field in this target compared to other targets. The implementation of the special case can be observed in the `HelmChartOutputPathField` implementation:

```python
def value_or_default(self, *, file_ending: str | None) -> str:
      if self.value:
          return self.value
      return os.path.join(self.address.spec_path.replace(os.sep, "."))
```

This special case could be removed by slightly modifying the signature of the `valur_or_default` method in the base `OutputPathField` class so it can take an optional `file_suffix` argument (which in this case would represent the chart version). With this modification, the `value_or_default` method would be used as such:

```python
artifact_path = field_set.output_path.value_or_default(file_suffix=chart.metadata.version, file_ending="tgz")
```

An alternative to this could be not consider file suffixes at all and use what `output_path` tells us as the final file name. In this case when the `publish` goal gets implemented, then a file rename would need to be done before the actual upload of the package artifact as the previously mentioned artifact name format is a Helm strict requirement as mentioned here:
https://helm.sh/docs/topics/registries/

> It might be useful to see what the experience is if we don’t leave off Computed. (As long as they are Optional or Required).

Anything that is optional or required does come back in the inputs; we just remove them when we process the result of the diff. End-to-end the process looks like this:

1. Read the current inputs and state from the provider
2. Diff an empty set of inputs against the current inputs + state
3. If there are diffs, copy the differing properties into the inputs
4. Generate the resource's definition using the inputs from (3)

When I originally wrote this up, I did not do (2) and (3). Instead, I used the inputs from (1) in (4). This resulted in very large definitions that at least in some cases--probably `aws:ec2:Instance` or `aws:s3:Bucket`--did not successfully `Check` due to the presence of conflicting input properties. For example, this can happen if there are two in/out properties A and B where if A is an input B is computed and vice-versa. The experience of copy-pasting a definition that didn't successfully import (and would not successfully create) is what led me to the current approach.

> The current experience of underspecifying feels less “correct” than overspecifying. Though it would be interesting to hand validate a number of concrete cases to better characterize the trade off.

FWIW, the current experience is consistent with what a user would need to do manually in order for an import to succeed.

It's called `description` in PEP 621, as well as all of the major tools' legacy metadata config, so you're already good there—only core metadata calls it `summary`. Similarly, `classifier` is called `classifiers` in all of them—its just not plural in core metadata because its represented as a multi-use field rather than an array (due to how RFC 2822 works).

What Setuptools calls `project_urls` is called `urls` in PEP 621 and all the other tools, and the legacy `home_page` (what setuptools calls `url`) is not explicitly specified at all in them (though it can be implicitly filled in by tools based on the `urls` value, IIRC).

There are some other things not handled here. What core metadata calls `Description` and setuptools calls `long_description` is called `readme` in PEP 621 and Poetry, and `description-file` in Flit. Also, `Requires-Python` in Core Metadata is `requires-python` in PEP 621 and Flit, but is `python_requires` in setuptools, and dependencies and entry points are rather different as well. See [PEP 621](https://peps.python.org/pep-0621/) for the full details.

@mlissner , the current architecture _**is able to process JSON responses**_ in method `_return_response_text_object`:
https://github.com/freelawproject/juriscraper/blob/23e61f8a391c9d3c68b9194168c9ee10e6a7ae85/juriscraper/AbstractSite.py#L382-L388

What is missing is that the optional parameters for page requests in dictionary `self.parameters` are _**only included when the requests is made with POST**_ method:
https://github.com/freelawproject/juriscraper/blob/23e61f8a391c9d3c68b9194168c9ee10e6a7ae85/juriscraper/AbstractSite.py#L359-L369

To solve this kind of issues, is necessary to also add the parameter `data` in the **GET** request in method `_request_url_get`:
https://github.com/freelawproject/juriscraper/blob/23e61f8a391c9d3c68b9194168c9ee10e6a7ae85/juriscraper/AbstractSite.py#L346-L357

```
def _request_url_get(self, url):
    """Execute GET request and assign appropriate request dictionary
    values
    """
    self.request["url"] = url
    self.request["response"] = self.request["session"].get(
        url,
        headers=self.request["headers"],
        verify=self.request["verify"],
        data=self.parameters,
        timeout=60,
        **self.request["parameters"],
    )
```

Use the specific `requests` exception was my 1st choice but then I saw the [hooks above](https://github.com/ministryofjustice/money-to-prisoners-common/blob/master/mtp_common/auth/api_client.py#L73) (which BTW I have no experience with) and I was wondering if this may also raise something else.

I know catch all exceptions is not exactly a best practice but I thought given the only code in the `try` block is an HTTP request it would be fine.

I suppose I could:
1. only catch [`requests.RequestException`](https://github.com/psf/requests/blob/master/requests/exceptions.py) (from which all `requests`'s exceptions inherit) and if anything else gets raised and not retried, it would just be raised (assuming I understand Python's exception handling and the `try/except/else/finally` correctly)
2. catch `requests.RequestException` but also our exception...I just realised something!

**I just realised something** I didn't notice yesterday 🤦 , even [our exceptions here in `mtp-common`'s `auth.exceptions`](https://github.com/ministryofjustice/money-to-prisoners-common/blob/905023f9e99468dd90d984a8ecc1c25deb6c08ec/mtp_common/auth/exceptions.py) all inherit from [`requests.exceptions.HTTPError` which also **is** also a `requests.RequestException`](https://github.com/psf/requests/blob/master/requests/exceptions.py#L28). 

**So it should be safe to only catch `requests.RequestException`** and that should both catch exceptions coming from `requests` and out exceptions raised via the hooks.

@michael-k _technically_ yes according to PEP 484. However, for strict type checking (pyright specifically) it expects it to be explicitly stated as `Optional`. I'm not sure how mypy handles it - I stopped using it some time ago due to it having too many issues (that took too long to be fixed) and being incredibly slow.

From [pyright's docs](https://github.com/microsoft/pyright/blob/main/docs/configuration.md#type-check-diagnostics-settings):
> **strictParameterNoneValue** [boolean]: PEP 484 indicates that when a function parameter is assigned a default value of None, its type should implicitly be Optional even if the explicit type is not. When enabled, this rule requires that parameter type annotations use Optional explicitly in this case. The default value for this setting is 'true'.

Not having the explicit `Optional` in a library like this can cause issues for those that want to use strict type checking forcing them to allow implicit `Optional`. Troposphere is one of the libraries that forced me to disable **strictParameterNoneValue** in some of my projects and is why it's disabled in the configuration I copy/pasted in this PR - I forgot to remove it. I opted to be explicit in adding my annotations to avoid the potential of causing issues for others.

```
        try:

        except Exception as exc:

            span.set_exc_info(exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb)
            self.handle_exception_span(exc, span)
            reraise(exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb)
        finally:
            try:
                del scope["datadog"]["request_span"]
            except KeyError:
                pass

            span.finish()
```

See https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/pull/3799#discussion_r893903331 . I was worried about calling `span.finish()` multiple times, but it looks like only the first `finish` is used in the duration.

I would name it RE_HAS_UNICODE_WORD or something along those lines.

Also, I just noticed that we could use `re.search()` instead of `re.findall()` since we just need a single match and not all matches to determine if we should use the unicode word regex vs. the ascii one. Time of asterisk = 0.00313

<details><summary>Script for measurements</summary>

```python
import time
import numpy as np
from numpy.random import randn
import pandas

data = tuple([pandas.DataFrame(randn(10000)) for i in range(0, 100000)])

def func_asterisk_tuple(dat):
    def func(*tup):
        l1 = tup[: len(tup) // 2]
        l2 = tup[len(tup) // 2 :]

    func(*dat)


def func_tuple(dat):
    def func(tup):
        l1 = tup[: len(tup) // 2]
        l2 = tup[len(tup) // 2 :]

    func(dat)


t_asterisk = 0
t_tuple = 0
N = 10
for it in range(0, N):
    s = time.time()
    func_asterisk_tuple(data)
    t_asterisk += time.time() - s

    s = time.time()
    func_tuple(data)
    t_tuple += time.time() - s


print(f"Time of asterisk is {t_asterisk / N}")
print(f"Time of tuple is {t_tuple / N}")

```
</details>

This is raising an error and not raising a warning or logging at warning level. So if it appears that we don't have e.g. `iam:DeleteRole` granted on `*`, Toil will bail out and refuse to create the cluster, with no way to override it.

I don't think that's the behavior we want. For one thing, we aren't 100% confident that we're actually testing all the ways a user could have permission to do something, so there will be cases where we do have the permissions we need but we don't know it.

We're also checking for permission grants on `*` (i.e. all resources); there might be ways to grant a permission on less than `*` that will let Toil work, but right now Toil can't understand them and will bail out if it sees them.

We probably want the user to still be able to use Toil in those cases. That could look like some kind of `--force-permissions` flag or something to override the checks, or it could look like just making the checks produce warning-level log messages when they fail, and going on to attempt the things we think we can't do anyway. Or we could pause and make the user hit `y` or something.

@fmagin 

> I don't fully understand the problem itself yet

I'm glad you asked! 😂 We have two issues:
- NumPy arrays do double and triple duty in the library: they can represent an image (which can be 2D, 3D, nD, 2D+channels, ...), a set of coordinates, a set of spatial extents... So we need a way to distinguish between all these types, while still allowing bare NumPy arrays through no problem. Here NewType is indeed what I thought was the right solution, although it is not the right solution e.g. for distinguishing between multichannel and single channel arrays.
- The other issue we have is that for many functions (and we hope to continue to increase how many), not just NumPy arrays are allowed, but actually any array-like, if they implement `__array__` and maybe NEP-18 (`__array_function__`). In this case, NewType falls over because we actually need to use a protocol, and that protocol is defined by NumPy, and you can't use NewType with a protocol.

The problem with putting them on one line is what do you do with the next line? For example,

```cpp
my_really_fantastic_and_great_object_of_some_sort.def("count",
                                                      a_lambda_or_somethign_else_1,
                                                      a_lambda_or_somethign_else_2
);
```

Gives you potentially very little space, and most editors won't get the spacing right for you when you are writing it (since it's a variable indent).

```cpp
my_really_fantastic_and_great_object_of_some_sort.def("count",
    a_lambda_or_somethign_else_1,
    a_lambda_or_somethign_else_2
);
```

makes the "count" much harder to notice/read with the other items.

```cpp
my_really_fantastic_and_great_object_of_some_sort.def(
    "count",
    a_lambda_or_somethign_else_1,
    a_lambda_or_somethign_else_2
);
```

@leezu:  First of all, by replacing NDArray constructors followed by assign operators by some Init function we should see some run time improvement, just because in Init function we do exactly the same as it was done in the constructor, but not doing assigns AND not calling destructors after that.

But the main goal of this replacement was the simplification of the tracing the NDArray objects during the debugging process.

It's extremely difficult to do that when millions and millions of NDArray objects are constructed just to pass to each other some values stored in their members and are destructed after that. 

The tracing of data flows is much easy when corresponding NDArray is reinitialized and reused when needed.

Whit that approach, more than 90% of NDArray constructor calls were eliminated.

pandas.Timestamp string representation do not display timezone name at all.
```python
>>> dt = tarantool.Datetime(year=1970, month=1, day=1)
>>> str(dt)
'1970-01-01 00:00:00'
>>> dt = tarantool.Datetime(year=1970, month=1, day=1, tz='MSK')
>>> str(dt)
'1970-01-01 00:00:00+03:00'
>>> dt = tarantool.Datetime(year=1970, month=1, day=1, tz='Europe/Moscow')
>>> str(dt)
'1970-01-01 00:00:00+03:00'
```

In case of `__repr__`, we display honest internals that could be used to build the same Datetime object (as expected of `__repr__`)
```
>>> tarantool.Datetime(year=1970, month=1, day=1)
datetime: Timestamp('1970-01-01 00:00:00'), tz: ""
>>> tarantool.Datetime(year=1970, month=1, day=1, tz='MSK')
datetime: Timestamp('1970-01-01 00:00:00+0300', tz='pytz.FixedOffset(180)'), tz: "MSK"
>>> tarantool.Datetime(year=1970, month=1, day=1, tz='Europe/Moscow')
datetime: Timestamp('1970-01-01 00:00:00+0300', tz='Europe/Moscow'), tz: "Europe/Moscow"
```

Because from prediction perspective (ex: images) we just need to call the model with raw tensor, but there's complication when `feature_columns` and `keep_columns` involved. These fields with existing tests & examples are implemented with a pandas-only assumption, and that's what `convert_pandas_to_batch_type` does when handling single column tensor with pandas format.

Since numpy doesn't not always require a column, replicating the same behavior of `convert_pandas_to_batch_type` could lead to inconsistency in preprocessing, so you can think of this as a short version of `convert_numpy_to_batch_type` just for prediction.

note to reviewers: I don't totally understand *why* this change fixes #4667. At first I was worried that this function might suffer from a larger problem like "`lgb.cv()` does not respect values passed to `params`", but that is not true.

Consider the following example. Setting `min_gain_to_split` to a large number for the binary objective results in no splits, as expected.

```r
library(lightgbm)
data("agaricus.train")
dtrain <- lgb.Dataset(
    data = agaricus.train$data
    , label = agaricus.train$label
)
lgb.cv(
    params = list(
        min_gain_to_split = 100000.0
    )
    , data = dtrain
    , verbose = 0L
    , nrounds = 3L
    , nfold = 2L
    , obj = "binary"
)
```

The logs are full of the following

> [LightGBM] [Warning] No further splits with positive gain, best gain: -inf
[LightGBM] [Warning] Stopped training because there are no more leaves that meet the split requirements

Changing to `min_gain_to_split = 0.0`, those warnings go away and training produces trees with multiple leaves.

Due to the loop order, we get into this logic branch only when the same worker is present in both config sections. At this point, `config[worker_name]['config']` is always `config_system`, and `worker_config` is always `config_all`.

Before, it was updating the config_system values with the config_all values:

```
config[worker_name]['config'].update(worker_config)
```

This patch basically just reverses the prior order of operations. It first updates the config_all values (`worker_config`) with the config_system values (`config[worker_name]['config']`), and then replaces the config with the new dictionary.

(And yeah, this whole loop is confusing and probably needs a rewrite, but this was the least amount of change I could think of that would reverse this precedence...)

This is the reverse of the trick above.

On the first line we subtract in place from x2/y2 the x1y1. This give us width and height.
On the second line, we take the x1/y1, multiply it by 2, add the width/height and then divide by 2. Effectively we do:

`cx = (x1 * 2 + width) / 2 = x1 + width / 2 = x1 + (x2-x1)/2 = (x1 + x2)/2`

The division trick is again applied to ensure we handle the integers without further castings and in-place.

Ah, I think I might've misinterpreted the suggestion on #19032 (not a problem from my side, happy to keep iterating).

**Option A**: I was thinking we'd be doing something like this:

| release | supports lambdex? | supports zip? | default layout | deprecation warnings |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2.17 | ✅ | ❌ | lambdex | if `layout = "lambdex"` is implicit, tell people to set it to prep for 2.18 |
| 2.18 | ✅ | ✅ | zip | if `layout = "lambdex"` is set at all, tell people to remove it and switch to `zip` |
| 2.19 | ❌ | ✅ | zip | none, migration over (or maybe just about removing the `[lambdex]` section entirely) |

That is, this patch focused on just what we need to cherry-pick back to 2.17, but doesn't actually change the FaaS-packaging functionality itself, so having a `zip` option and telling people to use it will be misleading. 

The next step for #18879 is #19076, which implements the 2.18 behaviour (including adding a `zip` option and flipping the defaults over), but that's separate because it shouldn't be cherry picked.

(NB. #19022 / 2bc078b0252931ccd5140432a04f3328a0d32e3f missed the 2.17 branch, even without the revert #19071.)

**Option B**: I could also imagine something like:

| release | supports lambdex? | supports zip? | default layout | deprecation warnings |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2.17 | ✅ | ✅  | lambdex | if `layout = "lambdex"` is implicit, tell people to set it: recommend `zip`, but allow `lambdex` if they have to |
| 2.18 | ✅ | ✅ | zip | if `layout = "lambdex"` is set at all, tell people to remove it and switch to `zip` |
| 2.19 | ❌ | ✅ | zip | none, migration over (or maybe just about removing the `[lambdex]` section entirely) |

But, that would require cherry-picking _all_ the new FaaS-packaging functionality to the 2.17 branch, and that feels a bit risky, to me.

**Option C**: option B but without any cherry-picks: increment each version by one (i.e. start the deprecations in 2.18 and finish in 2.20).

I was thinking and implementing towards option A. Were you thinking of one of these, or something else?

It's a bit weird, but an example can explain.
In `PySide2`, if an host class which contains a `Signal` class attribute and a method called `emit`, whenever you call the `emit` method of the `Signal` attribute, the host class method `emit` get called.
As a result, you can have either mismatch in the `emit` parameters or infinite recursion.
Have a look at the attached script, in particular, the class method `emit` method has a second parameter to avoid the signature mismatch.
The script goes into an infinite loop.
If the questions is why `PySide2` behaves like that then I do not know, but this is the best compromise I found to keep things running.

GitHub has a read-only namespace. Here's how you can synchronize it:
```
git config --add remote.origin.fetch '+refs/pull/*/head:refs/pull/origin/*'
git fetch --all
```
(given that you point `origin` to this repo)

You cannot push there but you'd get the code locally.
To push you'll indeed need to add a remote for my work:
```
git add webknjaz git@github.com:webknjaz/proxy.py.git
git fetch --all
```
Alternatively, you can even push by the remote URL w/o adding it with a name:
```
git push git@github.com:webknjaz/proxy.py.git <local_branch>:<remote_fork_branch>
```

This is all possible with the `Allow edits by maintainers` checkbox on PRs.

But you can also just go to the Files tab in the PR, click on the edit button (:pencil2:), right on the GH UI. And commit from there: https://github.com/webknjaz/proxy.py/edit/maintenance/sphinx-make/Makefile?pr=%2Fabhinavsingh%2Fproxy.py%2Fpull%2F752.

And re your question about max precision, you're close -- the value in the n-th 0xF place is the proportional flow in that direction so you don't know how much really flows in that without knowing how much flows in all other directions. Here's some examples, and assume pixel directions are laid out like this:

321
4x0
567

Then here's some select MFD pixel values and their proportional flow directions
* 0x00000000 -- nodata
* 0x00000001 - 1 proportional unit of flow in the 0 direction with a total sum of 1 means 1/1 -- 100% flows in direction 0
* 0x10000001 - 1 proportional unit of flow in the 0 direction and 1 in in the 7th, so direction 0 gets 1/2 of the flow and direction 7 gets 1/2
* 0xF0000001 - 1 proportional unit of flow in the 0 direction and 15 in in the 7th, so direction 0 gets 1/16 of the flow and direction 7 gets 15/16
* 0xFFFFFFF1 - 1 proportional unit of flow in the 0 direction and 15 in in ALL other directions. So direction 0 gets a proportion of 1/106 (0.94% increment) and all other directions get 15/106 (14.15% increment). This also shows that the smallest increment possible with this configuration is 1/106. 

We thought that wasn't too bad of a precision with also being able to otherwise encode a complicated flow direction in a 32 bit int.

I struggled with this very thing myself. My thoughts tore in two different directions:
* I know my own use case is diffing before launching, so it seems reasonable that we'd want the default behavior to only include launchable stacks.
* It's called `launch_action` and it makes the most sense if that action only applies to the launch command and no other command. That's largely how I've written the docs. For example, `launch_action` is completely disregarded by the `create`, `update`, and `delete` commands, as well as all the other commands as well. So it felt the most "correct" to make it by default not apply to `diff` unless you tell the `diff` command that you want it to only diff the launchable stacks. 

At this point, I'm teetering on the edge here between these two. I feel like the first option (launchable by default) is more practical based upon use cases, but the second option (all by default) is more consistent with documentation and useage.
